error:
Command '['gemini', '-p', 'You are an expert in reversible programming languages, particularly Janus. You write correct, minimal Janus code.

---

You are an expert in reversible computing and the reversible programming language Janus.

Write a Janus procedure with the following signature:

    procedure gray(int n, int g)

The procedure should: Convert the binary number n to its Gray code: g must become n XOR floor(n/2) (the bitwise exclusive-or of n and n shifted right by one bit). g starts at 0 and n must remain unchanged. Assume n >= 0.

Example: when called with n=13, g=0, the expected result is n=13, g=11.

Output rules:
- Output ONLY the Janus procedure definition(s), starting with 'procedure'.
- Do NOT include main() or any global variable declarations.
- Do NOT include any explanations outside the code.
- Do NOT add any comments.
- Single-line comments start with //; multi-line comments start with /* and end with */.
The BNF of the Janus language is as follows:
```
<Program> ::= (<ProcedureDef>)* <MainProcedureDef>
<ProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" <IdentNonMain> "(" [ <Param> ("," <Param>)* ] ")" (<Stmt>)+
<MainProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" "main" "(" ")" (<MainVdecl>)* (<Stmt>)+

<Param> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "[" "]" ]

<MainVdecl> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "=" <Expr> ]  | "int" <Ident> "[" "]" [ "=" <ArrayLit> ]
<ArrayLit> ::= "{" <Expr> ("," <Expr>)* "}"

<LocalDecl> ::= "int" <Ident> "=" <Expr>
<LocalDeclList> ::= <LocalDecl> ("," <LocalDecl>)*

<Stmt>
  ::= <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
   | "if" <Expr> "then" (<Stmt>)+ "else" (<Stmt>)+ "fi" <Expr>
   | "from" <Expr> "do"   (<Stmt>)+ "loop" (<Stmt>)+ "until" <Expr>
   | "local" <LocalDeclList> (<Stmt>)+ "delocal" <LocalDeclList>
   | "call" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | "uncall" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | <Lval> "<=>" <Lval>
   | "skip"

<ModOp> ::= "+=" | "-=" | "^=" | "*=" | "/="

<ArgList> ::= <Expr> ("," <Expr>)*

<Lval> ::= <Ident> [ "[" <Expr> "]" ]

<Expr> ::= <Expr> <BinOp> <Expr> | ("!" | "~") <Expr> | <Term>
<Term> ::= "(" <Expr> ")" | <Integer> | <Lval>
<BinOp> ::= "*" | "/" | "%" | "+" | "-" | "<=" | "<" | ">=" | ">" | "=" | "!=" | "^" | "&&" | "||"

<Ident> ::= <letter> (<alphaNum> | "_" | "'")*
<IdentNonMain> ::= <Ident>
<Integer> ::= <decimal-integer>
```

Static Reversibility Constraints (well-formedness rules)

```
C1. Reversible assignment (non-self-referential update)
    For any statement:
        <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
    let Base(<Lval>) be the identifier at the root of the lval
    (i.e., Base(x)=x, Base(a[i])=a).
    Then Base(<Lval>) MUST NOT occur in <Expr> as an identifier
    (neither as a variable nor as an array base).

          Examples:
            OK:   i += 1
            OK:   a[i] += 1
            NG:   i += i
            NG:   a[i] += a[j]

C2. If/fi consistency (entry/exit condition discipline)
    For:
        if E_in then S1 else S2 fi E_out
    forward execution must satisfy:
      - If E_in is true: execute S1 and after S1, E_out must be true.
      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.
    (In particular, E_in and E_out must be chosen so that the branch taken
     forward can be reconstructed backward.)

C3. From/until loop consistency + execution order (strengthened)
    For:
        from E_in do S1 loop S2 until E_out

    Execution order (forward):
      1) Evaluate E_in. It MUST hold at loop entry.
      2) Execute S1 once (if absent, treat as skip).
      3) Evaluate E_out.
         - If E_out is true, terminate the loop immediately.
         - If E_out is false, execute S2 once (if absent, treat as skip),
           then evaluate E_in and it MUST be false, and go back to step (2).

C4. local/delocal discipline (static well-formedness)

    For any block:
        local D1, ..., Dk   S   delocal D1', ..., Dk'

    the following must hold:

      (a) Arity agreement:
          The number of declarations must be the same:
              k = k'.

      (b) Identifier agreement:
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

      (c) Scope discipline:
          The identifiers introduced by the local declarations
          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

    Intuition:
      The local/delocal construct introduces temporary variables
      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
      while the delocal declarations specify the values
      required to re-enter the block during backward execution.

C5. Scope / declared-identifier discipline
    A program may use only identifiers that are in scope.
      - In a procedure body: identifiers must be procedure parameters or locals
        introduced by an enclosing local/delocal block.
      - In main: identifiers must be declared by earlier <MainVdecl> or locals.
      - No implicit globals.
      - No shadowing: a local declaration MUST NOT reuse an identifier already in scope.

C6. delocal expected-value check (runtime)
    For:
        local int x = E_in   S   delocal int x = E_out
    runtime check at delocal:
      - Immediately before delocal, the implementation checks:
            x == Eval(E_out)
        If it fails, abort.
    Note: E_out need not equal E_in.

      Examples:
            NG:   E_in is x
            NG:   E_out is x

C7. Semantics of the reversible update operators:
    - `x *= e` multiplies x by e and is undone by `x /= e`; it requires e != 0.
    - `x /= e` requires e != 0 and that x is exactly divisible by e (no remainder).
```

Single-line comments start with //; multi-line comments start with /* and end with */.

Self-check before output: if any of the following holds, rewrite the Janus program from scratch before producing the output.
- The program does not conform to the BNF given above.
- A reversible update has its left-hand-side variable appearing on the right-hand side (e.g., x += x, x -= x, x *= x).
- A comment is written in any form other than // or /* ... */.
- A non-existent keyword such as 'end' is used.
- The keyword 'end' appears at the end of a procedure.

```
          are in scope only within S.

      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
```

Static Reversibility Constraints (well-formedness rules)

```
C1. Reversible assignment (non-self-referential update)
    For any statement:
        <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
    let Base(<Lval>) be the identifier at the root of the lval
    (i.e., Base(x)=x, Base(a[i])=a).
    Then Base(<Lval>) MUST NOT occur in <Expr> as an identifier
    (neither as a variable nor as an array base).

          Examples:
            OK:   i += 1
            OK:   a[i] += 1
            NG:   i += i
            NG:   a[i] += a[j]

C2. If/fi consistency (entry/exit condition discipline)
    For:
        if E_in then S1 else S2 fi E_out
    forward execution must satisfy:
      - If E_in is true: execute S1 and after S1, E_out must be true.
      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.
    (In particular, E_in and E_out must be chosen so that the branch taken
     forward can be reconstructed backward.)

C3. From/until loop consistency + execution order (strengthened)
    For:
        from E_in do S1 loop S2 until E_out

    Execution order (forward):
      1) Evaluate E_in. It MUST hold at loop entry.
      2) Execute S1 once (if absent, treat as skip).
      3) Evaluate E_out.
         - If E_out is true, terminate the loop immediately.
         - If E_out is false, execute S2 once (if absent, treat as skip),
           then evaluate E_in and it MUST be false, and go back to step (2).

C4. local/delocal discipline (static well-formedness)

    For any block:
        local D1, ..., Dk   S   delocal D1', ..., Dk'

    the following must hold:

      (a) Arity agreement:
          The number of declarations must be the same:
              k = k'.

      (b) Identifier agreement:
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

      (c) Scope discipline:
          The identifiers introduced by the local declarations
          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

    Intuition:
      The local/delocal construct introduces temporary variables
      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
      while the delocal declarations specify the values
      required to re-enter the block during backward execution.

C5. Scope / declared-identifier discipline
    A program may use only identifiers that are in scope.
      - In a procedure body: identifiers must be procedure parameters or locals
        introduced by an enclosing local/delocal block.
      - In main: identifiers must be declared by earlier <MainVdecl> or locals.
      - No implicit globals.
      - No shadowing: a local declaration MUST NOT reuse an identifier already in scope.

C6. delocal expected-value check (runtime)
    For:
        local int x = E_in   S   delocal int x = E_out
    runtime check at delocal:
      - Immediately before delocal, the implementation checks:
            x == Eval(E_out)
        If it fails, abort.
    Note: E_out need not equal E_in.

      Examples:
            NG:   E_in is x
            NG:   E_out is x

C7. Semantics of the reversible update operators:
    - `x *= e` multiplies x by e and is undone by `x /= e`; it requires e != 0.
    - `x /= e` requires e != 0 and that x is exactly divisible by e (no remainder).
```

You are an expert in reversible computing and the reversible programming language Janus.

Write a Janus procedure with the following signature:

    procedure ddec(int a[], int n)

The procedure should: Decode a delta-encoded array in place (the inverse of delta encoding): after execution, for every index i with 0 <= i <= n-1, a[i] must equal the prefix sum a[0] + a[1] + ... + a[i] of the original values. n must remain unchanged. Assume n >= 1 and the array has exactly n elements.

Example: when called with a=[3, 2, 4, 5], n=4, the expected result is a=[3, 5, 9, 14], n=4.

Output rules:
- Output ONLY the Janus procedure definition(s), starting with 'procedure'.
- Do NOT include main() or any global variable declarations.
- Do NOT include any explanations outside the code.
- Do NOT add any comments.
- Single-line comments start with //; multi-line comments start with /* and end with */.
The BNF of the Janus language is as follows:
```
<Program> ::= (<ProcedureDef>)* <MainProcedureDef>
<ProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" <IdentNonMain> "(" [ <Param> ("," <Param>)* ] ")" (<Stmt>)+
<MainProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" "main" "(" ")" (<MainVdecl>)* (<Stmt>)+

<Param> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "[" "]" ]

<MainVdecl> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "=" <Expr> ]  | "int" <Ident> "[" "]" [ "=" <ArrayLit> ]
<ArrayLit> ::= "{" <Expr> ("," <Expr>)* "}"

<LocalDecl> ::= "int" <Ident> "=" <Expr>
<LocalDeclList> ::= <LocalDecl> ("," <LocalDecl>)*

<Stmt>
  ::= <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
   | "if" <Expr> "then" (<Stmt>)+ "else" (<Stmt>)+ "fi" <Expr>
   | "from" <Expr> "do"   (<Stmt>)+ "loop" (<Stmt>)+ "until" <Expr>
   | "local" <LocalDeclList> (<Stmt>)+ "delocal" <LocalDeclList>
   | "call" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | "uncall" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | <Lval> "<=>" <Lval>
   | "skip"

<ModOp> ::= "+=" | "-=" | "^=" | "*=" | "/="

<ArgList> ::= <Expr> ("," <Expr>)*

<Lval> ::= <Ident> [ "[" <Expr> "]" ]

<Expr> ::= <Expr> <BinOp> <Expr> | ("!" | "~") <Expr> | <Term>
<Term> ::= "(" <Expr> ")" | <Integer> | <Lval>
<BinOp> ::= "*" | "/" | "%" | "+" | "-" | "<=" | "<" | ">=" | ">" | "=" | "!=" | "^" | "&&" | "||"

<Ident> ::= <letter> (<alphaNum> | "_" | "'")*
<IdentNonMain> ::= <Ident>
<Integer> ::= <decimal-integer>
```

Static Reversibility Constraints (well-formedness rules)

```
C1. Reversible assignment (non-self-referential update)
    For any statement:
        <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
    let Base(<Lval>) be the identifier at the root of the lval
    (i.e., Base(x)=x, Base(a[i])=a).
    Then Base(<Lval>) MUST NOT occur in <Expr> as an identifier
    (neither as a variable nor as an array base).

          Examples:
            OK:   i += 1
            OK:   a[i] += 1
            NG:   i += i
            NG:   a[i] += a[j]

C2. If/fi consistency (entry/exit condition discipline)
    For:
        if E_in then S1 else S2 fi E_out
    forward execution must satisfy:
      - If E_in is true: execute S1 and after S1, E_out must be true.
      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.
    (In particular, E_in and E_out must be chosen so that the branch taken
     forward can be reconstructed backward.)

C3. From/until loop consistency + execution order (strengthened)
    For:
        from E_in do S1 loop S2 until E_out

    Execution order (forward):
      1) Evaluate E_in. It MUST hold at loop entry.
      2) Execute S1 once (if absent, treat as skip).
      3) Evaluate E_out.
         - If E_out is true, terminate the loop immediately.
         - If E_out is false, execute S2 once (if absent, treat as skip),
           then evaluate E_in and it MUST be false, and go back to step (2).

C4. local/delocal discipline (static well-formedness)

    For any block:
        local D1, ..., Dk   S   delocal D1', ..., Dk'

    the following must hold:

      (a) Arity agreement:
          The number of declarations must be the same:
              k = k'.

      (b) Identifier agreement:
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

      (c) Scope discipline:
          The identifiers introduced by the local declarations
          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

    Intuition:
      The local/delocal construct introduces temporary variables
      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
      while the delocal declarations specify the values
      required to re-enter the block during backward execution.

C5. Scope / declared-identifier discipline
    A program may use only identifiers that are in scope.
      - In a procedure body: identifiers must be procedure parameters or locals
        introduced by an enclosing local/delocal block.
      - In main: identifiers must be declared by earlier <MainVdecl> or locals.
      - No implicit globals.
      - No shadowing: a local declaration MUST NOT reuse an identifier already in scope.

C6. delocal expected-value check (runtime)
    For:
        local int x = E_in   S   delocal int x = E_out
    runtime check at delocal:
      - Immediately before delocal, the implementation checks:
            x == Eval(E_out)
        If it fails, abort.
    Note: E_out need not equal E_in.

      Examples:
            NG:   E_in is x
            NG:   E_out is x

C7. Semantics of the reversible update operators:
    - `x *= e` multiplies x by e and is undone by `x /= e`; it requires e != 0.
    - `x /= e` requires e != 0 and that x is exactly divisible by e (no remainder).
```

Single-line comments start with //; multi-line comments start with /* and end with */.

Self-check before output: if any of the following holds, rewrite the Janus program from scratch before producing the output.
- The program does not conform to the BNF given above.
- A reversible update has its left-hand-side variable appearing on the right-hand side (e.g., x += x, x -= x, x *= x).
- A comment is written in any form other than // or /* ... */.
- A non-existent keyword such as 'end' is used.
- The keyword 'end' appears at the end of a procedure.

```
<MainVdecl> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "=" <Expr> ]  | "int" <Ident> "[" "]" [ "=" <ArrayLit> ]

<LocalDecl> ::= "int" <Ident> "=" <Expr>
<LocalDeclList> ::= <LocalDecl> ("," <LocalDecl>)*

<Stmt>
  ::= <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
   | "if" <Expr> "then" (<Stmt>)+ "else" (<Stmt>)+ "fi" <Expr>

   | "local" <LocalDeclList> (<Stmt>)+ "delocal" <LocalDeclList>
   | "call" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | "uncall" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | <Lval> "<=>" <Lval>
   | "skip"

<ModOp> ::= "+=" | "-=" | "^=" | "*=" | "/="

<ArgList> ::= <Expr> ("," <Expr>)*

<Lval> ::= <Ident> [ "[" <Expr> "]" ]

<Expr> ::= <Expr> <BinOp> <Expr> | ("!" | "~") <Expr> | <Term>
```

```
          Examples:

    For:

      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.

     forward can be reconstructed backward.)

          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
```

```
   | "if" <Expr> "then" (<Stmt>)+ "else" (<Stmt>)+ "fi" <Expr>
   | "from" <Expr> "do"   (<Stmt>)+ "loop" (<Stmt>)+ "until" <Expr>
   | "local" <LocalDeclList> (<Stmt>)+ "delocal" <LocalDeclList>
   | "call" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | "uncall" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | <Lval> "<=>" <Lval>
   | "skip"

<ModOp> ::= "+=" | "-=" | "^=" | "*=" | "/="

<ArgList> ::= <Expr> ("," <Expr>)*

<Lval> ::= <Ident> [ "[" <Expr> "]" ]

<Expr> ::= <Expr> <BinOp> <Expr> | ("!" | "~") <Expr> | <Term>
<Term> ::= "(" <Expr> ")" | <Integer> | <Lval>
<BinOp> ::= "*" | "/" | "%" | "+" | "-" | "<=" | "<" | ">=" | ">" | "=" | "!=" | "^" | "&&" | "||"

<Ident> ::= <letter> (<alphaNum> | "_" | "'")*
<IdentNonMain> ::= <Ident>
<Integer> ::= <decimal-integer>
```

Static Reversibility Constraints (well-formedness rules)

```
C1. Reversible assignment (non-self-referential update)
    For any statement:
        <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
    let Base(<Lval>) be the identifier at the root of the lval
    (i.e., Base(x)=x, Base(a[i])=a).
    Then Base(<Lval>) MUST NOT occur in <Expr> as an identifier
    (neither as a variable nor as an array base).

          Examples:
            OK:   i += 1
            OK:   a[i] += 1
            NG:   i += i
            NG:   a[i] += a[j]

C2. If/fi consistency (entry/exit condition discipline)
    For:
        if E_in then S1 else S2 fi E_out
    forward execution must satisfy:
      - If E_in is true: execute S1 and after S1, E_out must be true.
      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.
    (In particular, E_in and E_out must be chosen so that the branch taken
     forward can be reconstructed backward.)

C3. From/until loop consistency + execution order (strengthened)
    For:
        from E_in do S1 loop S2 until E_out

    Execution order (forward):
      1) Evaluate E_in. It MUST hold at loop entry.
      2) Execute S1 once (if absent, treat as skip).
      3) Evaluate E_out.
         - If E_out is true, terminate the loop immediately.
         - If E_out is false, execute S2 once (if absent, treat as skip),
           then evaluate E_in and it MUST be false, and go back to step (2).

C4. local/delocal discipline (static well-formedness)

    For any block:
        local D1, ..., Dk   S   delocal D1', ..., Dk'

    the following must hold:

      (a) Arity agreement:
          The number of declarations must be the same:
              k = k'.

      (b) Identifier agreement:
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

      (c) Scope discipline:
          The identifiers introduced by the local declarations
          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

    Intuition:
      The local/delocal construct introduces temporary variables
      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
      while the delocal declarations specify the values
      required to re-enter the block during backward execution.

C5. Scope / declared-identifier discipline
    A program may use only identifiers that are in scope.
      - In a procedure body: identifiers must be procedure parameters or locals
        introduced by an enclosing local/delocal block.
      - In main: identifiers must be declared by earlier <MainVdecl> or locals.
      - No implicit globals.
      - No shadowing: a local declaration MUST NOT reuse an identifier already in scope.

C6. delocal expected-value check (runtime)
    For:
        local int x = E_in   S   delocal int x = E_out
    runtime check at delocal:
      - Immediately before delocal, the implementation checks:
            x == Eval(E_out)
        If it fails, abort.
    Note: E_out need not equal E_in.

      Examples:
            NG:   E_in is x
            NG:   E_out is x

C7. Semantics of the reversible update operators:
    - `x *= e` multiplies x by e and is undone by `x /= e`; it requires e != 0.
    - `x /= e` requires e != 0 and that x is exactly divisible by e (no remainder).
```

```
<MainVdecl> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "=" <Expr> ]  | "int" <Ident> "[" "]" [ "=" <ArrayLit> ]
<ArrayLit> ::= "{" <Expr> ("," <Expr>)* "}"

<LocalDecl> ::= "int" <Ident> "=" <Expr>
<LocalDeclList> ::= <LocalDecl> ("," <LocalDecl>)*

<Stmt>
  ::= <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
   | "if" <Expr> "then" (<Stmt>)+ "else" (<Stmt>)+ "fi" <Expr>
   | "from" <Expr> "do"   (<Stmt>)+ "loop" (<Stmt>)+ "until" <Expr>

<ArgList> ::= <Expr> ("," <Expr>)*

<Lval> ::= <Ident> [ "[" <Expr> "]" ]

<Expr> ::= <Expr> <BinOp> <Expr> | ("!" | "~") <Expr> | <Term>
```

```
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.
```

Static Reversibility Constraints (well-formedness rules)

```
C1. Reversible assignment (non-self-referential update)

    Then Base(<Lval>) MUST NOT occur in <Expr> as an identifier
    (neither as a variable nor as an array base).

          Examples:
            OK:   i += 1
            OK:   a[i] += 1
            NG:   i += i
            NG:   a[i] += a[j]

C2. If/fi consistency (entry/exit condition discipline)
    For:
        if E_in then S1 else S2 fi E_out
    forward execution must satisfy:
      - If E_in is true: execute S1 and after S1, E_out must be true.
      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.
    (In particular, E_in and E_out must be chosen so that the branch taken
     forward can be reconstructed backward.)

C3. From/until loop consistency + execution order (strengthened)
    For:
        from E_in do S1 loop S2 until E_out

    Execution order (forward):
      1) Evaluate E_in. It MUST hold at loop entry.
      2) Execute S1 once (if absent, treat as skip).
      3) Evaluate E_out.
         - If E_out is true, terminate the loop immediately.
         - If E_out is false, execute S2 once (if absent, treat as skip),
           then evaluate E_in and it MUST be false, and go back to step (2).

C4. local/delocal discipline (static well-formedness)

    For any block:
        local D1, ..., Dk   S   delocal D1', ..., Dk'

    the following must hold:

      (a) Arity agreement:
          The number of declarations must be the same:
              k = k'.

      (b) Identifier agreement:
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

      (c) Scope discipline:
          The identifiers introduced by the local declarations
          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

    Intuition:
      The local/delocal construct introduces temporary variables
      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
      while the delocal declarations specify the values
      required to re-enter the block during backward execution.

C5. Scope / declared-identifier discipline
    A program may use only identifiers that are in scope.
      - In a procedure body: identifiers must be procedure parameters or locals

    - `x *= e` multiplies x by e and is undone by `x /= e`; it requires e != 0.
```

```
<MainProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" "main" "(" ")" (<MainVdecl>)* (<Stmt>)+

<Param> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "[" "]" ]

<MainVdecl> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "=" <Expr> ]  | "int" <Ident> "[" "]" [ "=" <ArrayLit> ]
<ArrayLit> ::= "{" <Expr> ("," <Expr>)* "}"

<LocalDecl> ::= "int" <Ident> "=" <Expr>
<LocalDeclList> ::= <LocalDecl> ("," <LocalDecl>)*

<Stmt>
  ::= <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
   | "if" <Expr> "then" (<Stmt>)+ "else" (<Stmt>)+ "fi" <Expr>
   | "from" <Expr> "do"   (<Stmt>)+ "loop" (<Stmt>)+ "until" <Expr>
   | "local" <LocalDeclList> (<Stmt>)+ "delocal" <LocalDeclList>
   | "call" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | "uncall" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | <Lval> "<=>" <Lval>
   | "skip"

<ModOp> ::= "+=" | "-=" | "^=" | "*=" | "/="

<ArgList> ::= <Expr> ("," <Expr>)*

<Lval> ::= <Ident> [ "[" <Expr> "]" ]

<Expr> ::= <Expr> <BinOp> <Expr> | ("!" | "~") <Expr> | <Term>
<Term> ::= "(" <Expr> ")" | <Integer> | <Lval>
<BinOp> ::= "*" | "/" | "%" | "+" | "-" | "<=" | "<" | ">=" | ">" | "=" | "!=" | "^" | "&&" | "||"

<Ident> ::= <letter> (<alphaNum> | "_" | "'")*
<IdentNonMain> ::= <Ident>
<Integer> ::= <decimal-integer>
```

Static Reversibility Constraints (well-formedness rules)

```
C1. Reversible assignment (non-self-referential update)
    For any statement:
        <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
    let Base(<Lval>) be the identifier at the root of the lval
    (i.e., Base(x)=x, Base(a[i])=a).
    Then Base(<Lval>) MUST NOT occur in <Expr> as an identifier
    (neither as a variable nor as an array base).

          Examples:
            OK:   i += 1
            OK:   a[i] += 1
            NG:   i += i
            NG:   a[i] += a[j]

C2. If/fi consistency (entry/exit condition discipline)
    For:
        if E_in then S1 else S2 fi E_out
    forward execution must satisfy:
      - If E_in is true: execute S1 and after S1, E_out must be true.
      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.
    (In particular, E_in and E_out must be chosen so that the branch taken
     forward can be reconstructed backward.)

C3. From/until loop consistency + execution order (strengthened)
    For:
        from E_in do S1 loop S2 until E_out

    Execution order (forward):
      1) Evaluate E_in. It MUST hold at loop entry.
      2) Execute S1 once (if absent, treat as skip).
      3) Evaluate E_out.
         - If E_out is true, terminate the loop immediately.
         - If E_out is false, execute S2 once (if absent, treat as skip),
           then evaluate E_in and it MUST be false, and go back to step (2).

C4. local/delocal discipline (static well-formedness)

    For any block:
        local D1, ..., Dk   S   delocal D1', ..., Dk'

    the following must hold:

      (a) Arity agreement:
          The number of declarations must be the same:
              k = k'.

      (b) Identifier agreement:
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

      (c) Scope discipline:
          The identifiers introduced by the local declarations
          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

    Intuition:
      The local/delocal construct introduces temporary variables
      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
      while the delocal declarations specify the values
      required to re-enter the block during backward execution.

C5. Scope / declared-identifier discipline
    A program may use only identifiers that are in scope.
      - In a procedure body: identifiers must be procedure parameters or locals
        introduced by an enclosing local/delocal block.
      - In main: identifiers must be declared by earlier <MainVdecl> or locals.
      - No implicit globals.
      - No shadowing: a local declaration MUST NOT reuse an identifier already in scope.

C6. delocal expected-value check (runtime)
    For:
        local int x = E_in   S   delocal int x = E_out
    runtime check at delocal:
      - Immediately before delocal, the implementation checks:
            x == Eval(E_out)
        If it fails, abort.
    Note: E_out need not equal E_in.

      Examples:
            NG:   E_in is x
            NG:   E_out is x

C7. Semantics of the reversible update operators:
    - `x *= e` multiplies x by e and is undone by `x /= e`; it requires e != 0.
    - `x /= e` requires e != 0 and that x is exactly divisible by e (no remainder).
```

The procedure should: Rotate the first n elements of the array a to the left by k positions in place: for every index i with 0 <= i < n, a[i] must become the original element at index (i+k) mod n. n and k must remain unchanged. Assume 0 < k < n and the array has exactly n elements.

Example: when called with a=[1, 2, 3, 4, 5], n=5, k=2, the expected result is a=[3, 4, 5, 1, 2], n=5, k=2.

Output rules:
- Output ONLY the Janus procedure definition(s), starting with 'procedure'.
- Do NOT include main() or any global variable declarations.
- Do NOT include any explanations outside the code.
- Do NOT add any comments.
- Single-line comments start with //; multi-line comments start with /* and end with */.
The BNF of the Janus language is as follows:
```
<Program> ::= (<ProcedureDef>)* <MainProcedureDef>
<ProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" <IdentNonMain> "(" [ <Param> ("," <Param>)* ] ")" (<Stmt>)+
<MainProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" "main" "(" ")" (<MainVdecl>)* (<Stmt>)+

<Param> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "[" "]" ]

<MainVdecl> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "=" <Expr> ]  | "int" <Ident> "[" "]" [ "=" <ArrayLit> ]
<ArrayLit> ::= "{" <Expr> ("," <Expr>)* "}"

<LocalDecl> ::= "int" <Ident> "=" <Expr>
<LocalDeclList> ::= <LocalDecl> ("," <LocalDecl>)*

<Stmt>
  ::= <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
   | "if" <Expr> "then" (<Stmt>)+ "else" (<Stmt>)+ "fi" <Expr>
   | "from" <Expr> "do"   (<Stmt>)+ "loop" (<Stmt>)+ "until" <Expr>
   | "local" <LocalDeclList> (<Stmt>)+ "delocal" <LocalDeclList>
   | "call" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | "uncall" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | <Lval> "<=>" <Lval>
   | "skip"

<ModOp> ::= "+=" | "-=" | "^=" | "*=" | "/="

<ArgList> ::= <Expr> ("," <Expr>)*

<Lval> ::= <Ident> [ "[" <Expr> "]" ]

<Expr> ::= <Expr> <BinOp> <Expr> | ("!" | "~") <Expr> | <Term>
<Term> ::= "(" <Expr> ")" | <Integer> | <Lval>
<BinOp> ::= "*" | "/" | "%" | "+" | "-" | "<=" | "<" | ">=" | ">" | "=" | "!=" | "^" | "&&" | "||"

<Ident> ::= <letter> (<alphaNum> | "_" | "'")*
<IdentNonMain> ::= <Ident>
<Integer> ::= <decimal-integer>
```

Static Reversibility Constraints (well-formedness rules)

```
C1. Reversible assignment (non-self-referential update)

        <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
    let Base(<Lval>) be the identifier at the root of the lval
    (i.e., Base(x)=x, Base(a[i])=a).
    Then Base(<Lval>) MUST NOT occur in <Expr> as an identifier
    (neither as a variable nor as an array base).

          Examples:
            OK:   i += 1
            OK:   a[i] += 1
            NG:   i += i
            NG:   a[i] += a[j]

C2. If/fi consistency (entry/exit condition discipline)
    For:
        if E_in then S1 else S2 fi E_out
    forward execution must satisfy:
      - If E_in is true: execute S1 and after S1, E_out must be true.
      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.
    (In particular, E_in and E_out must be chosen so that the branch taken
     forward can be reconstructed backward.)

C3. From/until loop consistency + execution order (strengthened)
    For:
        from E_in do S1 loop S2 until E_out

    Execution order (forward):
      1) Evaluate E_in. It MUST hold at loop entry.
      2) Execute S1 once (if absent, treat as skip).
      3) Evaluate E_out.
         - If E_out is true, terminate the loop immediately.
         - If E_out is false, execute S2 once (if absent, treat as skip),
           then evaluate E_in and it MUST be false, and go back to step (2).

C4. local/delocal discipline (static well-formedness)

    For any block:
        local D1, ..., Dk   S   delocal D1', ..., Dk'

    the following must hold:

      (a) Arity agreement:
          The number of declarations must be the same:
              k = k'.

      (b) Identifier agreement:
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

      (c) Scope discipline:
          The identifiers introduced by the local declarations
          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

    Intuition:
      The local/delocal construct introduces temporary variables
      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
      while the delocal declarations specify the values
      required to re-enter the block during backward execution.

C5. Scope / declared-identifier discipline
    A program may use only identifiers that are in scope.
      - In a procedure body: identifiers must be procedure parameters or locals
        introduced by an enclosing local/delocal block.
      - In main: identifiers must be declared by earlier <MainVdecl> or locals.
      - No implicit globals.
      - No shadowing: a local declaration MUST NOT reuse an identifier already in scope.

C6. delocal expected-value check (runtime)
    For:
        local int x = E_in   S   delocal int x = E_out
    runtime check at delocal:
      - Immediately before delocal, the implementation checks:
            x == Eval(E_out)
        If it fails, abort.
    Note: E_out need not equal E_in.

      Examples:
            NG:   E_in is x
            NG:   E_out is x

C7. Semantics of the reversible update operators:
    - `x *= e` multiplies x by e and is undone by `x /= e`; it requires e != 0.
    - `x /= e` requires e != 0 and that x is exactly divisible by e (no remainder).
```

You are an expert in reversible computing and the reversible programming language Janus.

Write a Janus procedure with the following signature:

    procedure denc(int a[], int n)

The procedure should: Delta-encode the array a of n elements in place: after execution, for every index i with 1 <= i <= n-1, a[i] must equal the difference (original a[i]) - (original a[i-1]). a[0] and n must remain unchanged. Assume n >= 1 and the array has exactly n elements.

Example: when called with a=[3, 5, 9, 14], n=4, the expected result is a=[3, 2, 4, 5], n=4.

Output rules:
- Output ONLY the Janus procedure definition(s), starting with 'procedure'.
- Do NOT include main() or any global variable declarations.
- Do NOT include any explanations outside the code.
- Do NOT add any comments.
- Single-line comments start with //; multi-line comments start with /* and end with */.
The BNF of the Janus language is as follows:
```
<Program> ::= (<ProcedureDef>)* <MainProcedureDef>
<ProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" <IdentNonMain> "(" [ <Param> ("," <Param>)* ] ")" (<Stmt>)+
<MainProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" "main" "(" ")" (<MainVdecl>)* (<Stmt>)+

<Param> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "[" "]" ]

<MainVdecl> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "=" <Expr> ]  | "int" <Ident> "[" "]" [ "=" <ArrayLit> ]
<ArrayLit> ::= "{" <Expr> ("," <Expr>)* "}"

<LocalDecl> ::= "int" <Ident> "=" <Expr>
<LocalDeclList> ::= <LocalDecl> ("," <LocalDecl>)*

<Stmt>
  ::= <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
   | "if" <Expr> "then" (<Stmt>)+ "else" (<Stmt>)+ "fi" <Expr>
   | "from" <Expr> "do"   (<Stmt>)+ "loop" (<Stmt>)+ "until" <Expr>
   | "local" <LocalDeclList> (<Stmt>)+ "delocal" <LocalDeclList>
   | "call" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | "uncall" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | <Lval> "<=>" <Lval>
   | "skip"

<ModOp> ::= "+=" | "-=" | "^=" | "*=" | "/="

<ArgList> ::= <Expr> ("," <Expr>)*

<Lval> ::= <Ident> [ "[" <Expr> "]" ]

<Expr> ::= <Expr> <BinOp> <Expr> | ("!" | "~") <Expr> | <Term>
<Term> ::= "(" <Expr> ")" | <Integer> | <Lval>
<BinOp> ::= "*" | "/" | "%" | "+" | "-" | "<=" | "<" | ">=" | ">" | "=" | "!=" | "^" | "&&" | "||"

<Ident> ::= <letter> (<alphaNum> | "_" | "'")*
<IdentNonMain> ::= <Ident>
<Integer> ::= <decimal-integer>
```

Static Reversibility Constraints (well-formedness rules)

```
C1. Reversible assignment (non-self-referential update)

        <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
    let Base(<Lval>) be the identifier at the root of the lval
    (i.e., Base(x)=x, Base(a[i])=a).
    Then Base(<Lval>) MUST NOT occur in <Expr> as an identifier
    (neither as a variable nor as an array base).

          Examples:
            OK:   i += 1
            OK:   a[i] += 1
            NG:   i += i
            NG:   a[i] += a[j]

C2. If/fi consistency (entry/exit condition discipline)
    For:
        if E_in then S1 else S2 fi E_out
    forward execution must satisfy:
      - If E_in is true: execute S1 and after S1, E_out must be true.
      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.
    (In particular, E_in and E_out must be chosen so that the branch taken
     forward can be reconstructed backward.)

C3. From/until loop consistency + execution order (strengthened)
    For:
        from E_in do S1 loop S2 until E_out

    Execution order (forward):
      1) Evaluate E_in. It MUST hold at loop entry.
      2) Execute S1 once (if absent, treat as skip).
      3) Evaluate E_out.
         - If E_out is true, terminate the loop immediately.
         - If E_out is false, execute S2 once (if absent, treat as skip),
           then evaluate E_in and it MUST be false, and go back to step (2).

C4. local/delocal discipline (static well-formedness)

    For any block:
        local D1, ..., Dk   S   delocal D1', ..., Dk'

    the following must hold:

      (a) Arity agreement:
          The number of declarations must be the same:
              k = k'.

      (b) Identifier agreement:
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

      (c) Scope discipline:
          The identifiers introduced by the local declarations
          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

    Intuition:
      The local/delocal construct introduces temporary variables
      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
      while the delocal declarations specify the values
      required to re-enter the block during backward execution.

C5. Scope / declared-identifier discipline
    A program may use only identifiers that are in scope.
      - In a procedure body: identifiers must be procedure parameters or locals
        introduced by an enclosing local/delocal block.
      - In main: identifiers must be declared by earlier <MainVdecl> or locals.
      - No implicit globals.
      - No shadowing: a local declaration MUST NOT reuse an identifier already in scope.

C6. delocal expected-value check (runtime)
    For:
        local int x = E_in   S   delocal int x = E_out
    runtime check at delocal:
      - Immediately before delocal, the implementation checks:
            x == Eval(E_out)
        If it fails, abort.
    Note: E_out need not equal E_in.

      Examples:
            NG:   E_in is x
            NG:   E_out is x

C7. Semantics of the reversible update operators:
    - `x *= e` multiplies x by e and is undone by `x /= e`; it requires e != 0.
    - `x /= e` requires e != 0 and that x is exactly divisible by e (no remainder).
```

```
          are in scope only within S.
```

```
   | "skip"
```

Static Reversibility Constraints (well-formedness rules)

```
C1. Reversible assignment (non-self-referential update)
    For any statement:
        <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
    let Base(<Lval>) be the identifier at the root of the lval
    (i.e., Base(x)=x, Base(a[i])=a).
    Then Base(<Lval>) MUST NOT occur in <Expr> as an identifier
    (neither as a variable nor as an array base).

          Examples:
            OK:   i += 1
            OK:   a[i] += 1
            NG:   i += i
            NG:   a[i] += a[j]

C2. If/fi consistency (entry/exit condition discipline)
    For:
        if E_in then S1 else S2 fi E_out
    forward execution must satisfy:
      - If E_in is true: execute S1 and after S1, E_out must be true.
      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.
    (In particular, E_in and E_out must be chosen so that the branch taken
     forward can be reconstructed backward.)

C3. From/until loop consistency + execution order (strengthened)
    For:
        from E_in do S1 loop S2 until E_out

    Execution order (forward):
      1) Evaluate E_in. It MUST hold at loop entry.
      2) Execute S1 once (if absent, treat as skip).
      3) Evaluate E_out.
         - If E_out is true, terminate the loop immediately.
         - If E_out is false, execute S2 once (if absent, treat as skip),
           then evaluate E_in and it MUST be false, and go back to step (2).

C4. local/delocal discipline (static well-formedness)

    For any block:
        local D1, ..., Dk   S   delocal D1', ..., Dk'

    the following must hold:

      (a) Arity agreement:
          The number of declarations must be the same:
              k = k'.

      (b) Identifier agreement:
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

      (c) Scope discipline:
          The identifiers introduced by the local declarations
          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

    Intuition:
      The local/delocal construct introduces temporary variables
      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
      while the delocal declarations specify the values
      required to re-enter the block during backward execution.

C5. Scope / declared-identifier discipline
    A program may use only identifiers that are in scope.
      - In a procedure body: identifiers must be procedure parameters or locals
        introduced by an enclosing local/delocal block.
      - In main: identifiers must be declared by earlier <MainVdecl> or locals.
      - No implicit globals.
      - No shadowing: a local declaration MUST NOT reuse an identifier already in scope.

C6. delocal expected-value check (runtime)
    For:
        local int x = E_in   S   delocal int x = E_out
    runtime check at delocal:
      - Immediately before delocal, the implementation checks:
            x == Eval(E_out)
        If it fails, abort.
    Note: E_out need not equal E_in.

      Examples:
            NG:   E_in is x
            NG:   E_out is x

C7. Semantics of the reversible update operators:
    - `x *= e` multiplies x by e and is undone by `x /= e`; it requires e != 0.
    - `x /= e` requires e != 0 and that x is exactly divisible by e (no remainder).
```

```
<Param> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "[" "]" ]

<MainVdecl> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "=" <Expr> ]  | "int" <Ident> "[" "]" [ "=" <ArrayLit> ]
<ArrayLit> ::= "{" <Expr> ("," <Expr>)* "}"

<LocalDecl> ::= "int" <Ident> "=" <Expr>
<LocalDeclList> ::= <LocalDecl> ("," <LocalDecl>)*

<Stmt>
  ::= <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
   | "if" <Expr> "then" (<Stmt>)+ "else" (<Stmt>)+ "fi" <Expr>

<ArgList> ::= <Expr> ("," <Expr>)*

<Expr> ::= <Expr> <BinOp> <Expr> | ("!" | "~") <Expr> | <Term>
```

```
   | "skip"
```

Static Reversibility Constraints (well-formedness rules)

```
C1. Reversible assignment (non-self-referential update)

        <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
    let Base(<Lval>) be the identifier at the root of the lval
    (i.e., Base(x)=x, Base(a[i])=a).
    Then Base(<Lval>) MUST NOT occur in <Expr> as an identifier
    (neither as a variable nor as an array base).

          Examples:
            OK:   i += 1
            OK:   a[i] += 1
            NG:   i += i
            NG:   a[i] += a[j]

C2. If/fi consistency (entry/exit condition discipline)
    For:
        if E_in then S1 else S2 fi E_out
    forward execution must satisfy:
      - If E_in is true: execute S1 and after S1, E_out must be true.
      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.
    (In particular, E_in and E_out must be chosen so that the branch taken
     forward can be reconstructed backward.)

    For:
        from E_in do S1 loop S2 until E_out

    Execution order (forward):
      1) Evaluate E_in. It MUST hold at loop entry.
      2) Execute S1 once (if absent, treat as skip).
      3) Evaluate E_out.
         - If E_out is true, terminate the loop immediately.
         - If E_out is false, execute S2 once (if absent, treat as skip),

C4. local/delocal discipline (static well-formedness)

    For any block:
        local D1, ..., Dk   S   delocal D1', ..., Dk'

    the following must hold:

      (a) Arity agreement:

              k = k'.

      (b) Identifier agreement:
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

      (c) Scope discipline:
          The identifiers introduced by the local declarations
          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

    Intuition:
      The local/delocal construct introduces temporary variables
      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
      while the delocal declarations specify the values
      required to re-enter the block during backward execution.

C5. Scope / declared-identifier discipline
    A program may use only identifiers that are in scope.
      - In a procedure body: identifiers must be procedure parameters or locals
        introduced by an enclosing local/delocal block.
      - In main: identifiers must be declared by earlier <MainVdecl> or locals.
      - No implicit globals.
      - No shadowing: a local declaration MUST NOT reuse an identifier already in scope.

C6. delocal expected-value check (runtime)
    For:

      - Immediately before delocal, the implementation checks:
            x == Eval(E_out)
        If it fails, abort.
    Note: E_out need not equal E_in.

      Examples:
            NG:   E_in is x
            NG:   E_out is x

C7. Semantics of the reversible update operators:
    - `x *= e` multiplies x by e and is undone by `x /= e`; it requires e != 0.
```

```
<MainProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" "main" "(" ")" (<MainVdecl>)* (<Stmt>)+

<Param> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "[" "]" ]

<MainVdecl> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "=" <Expr> ]  | "int" <Ident> "[" "]" [ "=" <ArrayLit> ]
<ArrayLit> ::= "{" <Expr> ("," <Expr>)* "}"

<LocalDecl> ::= "int" <Ident> "=" <Expr>
<LocalDeclList> ::= <LocalDecl> ("," <LocalDecl>)*

<Stmt>
  ::= <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
   | "if" <Expr> "then" (<Stmt>)+ "else" (<Stmt>)+ "fi" <Expr>
   | "from" <Expr> "do"   (<Stmt>)+ "loop" (<Stmt>)+ "until" <Expr>
   | "local" <LocalDeclList> (<Stmt>)+ "delocal" <LocalDeclList>
   | "call" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | "uncall" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | <Lval> "<=>" <Lval>
   | "skip"

<ModOp> ::= "+=" | "-=" | "^=" | "*=" | "/="

<ArgList> ::= <Expr> ("," <Expr>)*

<Lval> ::= <Ident> [ "[" <Expr> "]" ]

<Expr> ::= <Expr> <BinOp> <Expr> | ("!" | "~") <Expr> | <Term>
<Term> ::= "(" <Expr> ")" | <Integer> | <Lval>
<BinOp> ::= "*" | "/" | "%" | "+" | "-" | "<=" | "<" | ">=" | ">" | "=" | "!=" | "^" | "&&" | "||"
```

```
            NG:   a[i] += a[j]

    For:
        if E_in then S1 else S2 fi E_out

      - If E_in is true: execute S1 and after S1, E_out must be true.
      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.

        local D1, ..., Dk   S   delocal D1', ..., Dk'

    the following must hold:

              k = k'.

      (b) Identifier agreement:
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

      (c) Scope discipline:
          The identifiers introduced by the local declarations
          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
      while the delocal declarations specify the values
      required to re-enter the block during backward execution.

    Note: E_out need not equal E_in.

      Examples:
            NG:   E_in is x
            NG:   E_out is x
```

```
<Program> ::= (<ProcedureDef>)* <MainProcedureDef>
<ProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" <IdentNonMain> "(" [ <Param> ("," <Param>)* ] ")" (<Stmt>)+
<MainProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" "main" "(" ")" (<MainVdecl>)* (<Stmt>)+

<Param> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "[" "]" ]

<MainVdecl> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "=" <Expr> ]  | "int" <Ident> "[" "]" [ "=" <ArrayLit> ]
<ArrayLit> ::= "{" <Expr> ("," <Expr>)* "}"

<LocalDecl> ::= "int" <Ident> "=" <Expr>
<LocalDeclList> ::= <LocalDecl> ("," <LocalDecl>)*

<Stmt>
  ::= <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
   | "if" <Expr> "then" (<Stmt>)+ "else" (<Stmt>)+ "fi" <Expr>
   | "from" <Expr> "do"   (<Stmt>)+ "loop" (<Stmt>)+ "until" <Expr>
   | "local" <LocalDeclList> (<Stmt>)+ "delocal" <LocalDeclList>
   | "call" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | "uncall" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | <Lval> "<=>" <Lval>
   | "skip"

<ModOp> ::= "+=" | "-=" | "^=" | "*=" | "/="

<ArgList> ::= <Expr> ("," <Expr>)*

<Lval> ::= <Ident> [ "[" <Expr> "]" ]

<Expr> ::= <Expr> <BinOp> <Expr> | ("!" | "~") <Expr> | <Term>
<Term> ::= "(" <Expr> ")" | <Integer> | <Lval>
<BinOp> ::= "*" | "/" | "%" | "+" | "-" | "<=" | "<" | ">=" | ">" | "=" | "!=" | "^" | "&&" | "||"

<Ident> ::= <letter> (<alphaNum> | "_" | "'")*
<IdentNonMain> ::= <Ident>
<Integer> ::= <decimal-integer>
```

Static Reversibility Constraints (well-formedness rules)

```
C1. Reversible assignment (non-self-referential update)
    For any statement:
        <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
    let Base(<Lval>) be the identifier at the root of the lval
    (i.e., Base(x)=x, Base(a[i])=a).
    Then Base(<Lval>) MUST NOT occur in <Expr> as an identifier
    (neither as a variable nor as an array base).

          Examples:
            OK:   i += 1
            OK:   a[i] += 1
            NG:   i += i
            NG:   a[i] += a[j]

C2. If/fi consistency (entry/exit condition discipline)
    For:
        if E_in then S1 else S2 fi E_out
    forward execution must satisfy:
      - If E_in is true: execute S1 and after S1, E_out must be true.
      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.
    (In particular, E_in and E_out must be chosen so that the branch taken
     forward can be reconstructed backward.)

C3. From/until loop consistency + execution order (strengthened)
    For:
        from E_in do S1 loop S2 until E_out

    Execution order (forward):
      1) Evaluate E_in. It MUST hold at loop entry.
      2) Execute S1 once (if absent, treat as skip).
      3) Evaluate E_out.
         - If E_out is true, terminate the loop immediately.
         - If E_out is false, execute S2 once (if absent, treat as skip),
           then evaluate E_in and it MUST be false, and go back to step (2).

C4. local/delocal discipline (static well-formedness)

    For any block:
        local D1, ..., Dk   S   delocal D1', ..., Dk'

    the following must hold:

      (a) Arity agreement:
          The number of declarations must be the same:
              k = k'.

      (b) Identifier agreement:
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

      (c) Scope discipline:
          The identifiers introduced by the local declarations
          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

    Intuition:
      The local/delocal construct introduces temporary variables
      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
      while the delocal declarations specify the values
      required to re-enter the block during backward execution.

C5. Scope / declared-identifier discipline
    A program may use only identifiers that are in scope.
      - In a procedure body: identifiers must be procedure parameters or locals
        introduced by an enclosing local/delocal block.
      - In main: identifiers must be declared by earlier <MainVdecl> or locals.
      - No implicit globals.
      - No shadowing: a local declaration MUST NOT reuse an identifier already in scope.

C6. delocal expected-value check (runtime)
    For:
        local int x = E_in   S   delocal int x = E_out
    runtime check at delocal:
      - Immediately before delocal, the implementation checks:
            x == Eval(E_out)
        If it fails, abort.
    Note: E_out need not equal E_in.

      Examples:
            NG:   E_in is x
            NG:   E_out is x

C7. Semantics of the reversible update operators:
    - `x *= e` multiplies x by e and is undone by `x /= e`; it requires e != 0.
    - `x /= e` requires e != 0 and that x is exactly divisible by e (no remainder).
```

The procedure should: Encode the pair (x, y) into a single number with the Cantor pairing function: z must become (x+y)*(x+y+1)/2 + y. z starts at 0. After execution x and y must both be 0 (the pair is consumed into z; the pairing is a bijection, so no information is lost). Assume x >= 0 and y >= 0.

Example: when called with x=2, y=3, z=0, the expected result is x=0, y=0, z=18.

Output rules:
- Output ONLY the Janus procedure definition(s), starting with 'procedure'.
- Do NOT include main() or any global variable declarations.
- Do NOT include any explanations outside the code.
- Do NOT add any comments.
- Single-line comments start with //; multi-line comments start with /* and end with */.

```
<Program> ::= (<ProcedureDef>)* <MainProcedureDef>
<ProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" <IdentNonMain> "(" [ <Param> ("," <Param>)* ] ")" (<Stmt>)+
<MainProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" "main" "(" ")" (<MainVdecl>)* (<Stmt>)+

<Param> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "[" "]" ]

<MainVdecl> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "=" <Expr> ]  | "int" <Ident> "[" "]" [ "=" <ArrayLit> ]
<ArrayLit> ::= "{" <Expr> ("," <Expr>)* "}"

<LocalDecl> ::= "int" <Ident> "=" <Expr>
<LocalDeclList> ::= <LocalDecl> ("," <LocalDecl>)*

<Stmt>
  ::= <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
   | "if" <Expr> "then" (<Stmt>)+ "else" (<Stmt>)+ "fi" <Expr>
   | "from" <Expr> "do"   (<Stmt>)+ "loop" (<Stmt>)+ "until" <Expr>
   | "local" <LocalDeclList> (<Stmt>)+ "delocal" <LocalDeclList>
   | "call" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | "uncall" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | <Lval> "<=>" <Lval>
   | "skip"

<ModOp> ::= "+=" | "-=" | "^=" | "*=" | "/="

<ArgList> ::= <Expr> ("," <Expr>)*

<Lval> ::= <Ident> [ "[" <Expr> "]" ]

<Expr> ::= <Expr> <BinOp> <Expr> | ("!" | "~") <Expr> | <Term>
<Term> ::= "(" <Expr> ")" | <Integer> | <Lval>
<BinOp> ::= "*" | "/" | "%" | "+" | "-" | "<=" | "<" | ">=" | ">" | "=" | "!=" | "^" | "&&" | "||"

<Ident> ::= <letter> (<alphaNum> | "_" | "'")*
<IdentNonMain> ::= <Ident>
<Integer> ::= <decimal-integer>
```

Static Reversibility Constraints (well-formedness rules)

```
C1. Reversible assignment (non-self-referential update)
    For any statement:
        <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
    let Base(<Lval>) be the identifier at the root of the lval
    (i.e., Base(x)=x, Base(a[i])=a).
    Then Base(<Lval>) MUST NOT occur in <Expr> as an identifier
    (neither as a variable nor as an array base).

          Examples:
            OK:   i += 1
            OK:   a[i] += 1
            NG:   i += i
            NG:   a[i] += a[j]

C2. If/fi consistency (entry/exit condition discipline)
    For:
        if E_in then S1 else S2 fi E_out
    forward execution must satisfy:
      - If E_in is true: execute S1 and after S1, E_out must be true.
      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.
    (In particular, E_in and E_out must be chosen so that the branch taken
     forward can be reconstructed backward.)

C3. From/until loop consistency + execution order (strengthened)
    For:
        from E_in do S1 loop S2 until E_out

    Execution order (forward):
      1) Evaluate E_in. It MUST hold at loop entry.
      2) Execute S1 once (if absent, treat as skip).
      3) Evaluate E_out.
         - If E_out is true, terminate the loop immediately.
         - If E_out is false, execute S2 once (if absent, treat as skip),
           then evaluate E_in and it MUST be false, and go back to step (2).

C4. local/delocal discipline (static well-formedness)

    For any block:
        local D1, ..., Dk   S   delocal D1', ..., Dk'

    the following must hold:

      (a) Arity agreement:
          The number of declarations must be the same:
              k = k'.

      (b) Identifier agreement:
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

      (c) Scope discipline:
          The identifiers introduced by the local declarations
          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

    Intuition:
      The local/delocal construct introduces temporary variables
      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
      while the delocal declarations specify the values
      required to re-enter the block during backward execution.

C5. Scope / declared-identifier discipline
    A program may use only identifiers that are in scope.
      - In a procedure body: identifiers must be procedure parameters or locals
        introduced by an enclosing local/delocal block.
      - In main: identifiers must be declared by earlier <MainVdecl> or locals.
      - No implicit globals.
      - No shadowing: a local declaration MUST NOT reuse an identifier already in scope.

C6. delocal expected-value check (runtime)
    For:

    runtime check at delocal:
      - Immediately before delocal, the implementation checks:
            x == Eval(E_out)
        If it fails, abort.
    Note: E_out need not equal E_in.

      Examples:
            NG:   E_in is x
            NG:   E_out is x

C7. Semantics of the reversible update operators:
    - `x *= e` multiplies x by e and is undone by `x /= e`; it requires e != 0.
    - `x /= e` requires e != 0 and that x is exactly divisible by e (no remainder).
```

Command '['gemini', '-p', 'You are an expert in reversible programming languages, particularly Janus. You write correct, minimal Janus code.

---

You are an expert in reversible computing and the reversible programming language Janus.

Write a Janus procedure with the following signature:

    procedure gray(int n, int g)

The procedure should: Convert the binary number n to its Gray code: g must become n XOR floor(n/2) (the bitwise exclusive-or of n and n shifted right by one bit). g starts at 0 and n must remain unchanged. Assume n >= 0.

Example: when called with n=13, g=0, the expected result is n=13, g=11.

Output rules:
- Output ONLY the Janus procedure definition(s), starting with 'procedure'.
- Do NOT include main() or any global variable declarations.
- Do NOT include any explanations outside the code.
- Do NOT add any comments.
- Single-line comments start with //; multi-line comments start with /* and end with */.
The BNF of the Janus language is as follows:
```
<Program> ::= (<ProcedureDef>)* <MainProcedureDef>
<ProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" <IdentNonMain> "(" [ <Param> ("," <Param>)* ] ")" (<Stmt>)+
<MainProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" "main" "(" ")" (<MainVdecl>)* (<Stmt>)+

<Param> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "[" "]" ]

<MainVdecl> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "=" <Expr> ]  | "int" <Ident> "[" "]" [ "=" <ArrayLit> ]
<ArrayLit> ::= "{" <Expr> ("," <Expr>)* "}"

<LocalDecl> ::= "int" <Ident> "=" <Expr>
<LocalDeclList> ::= <LocalDecl> ("," <LocalDecl>)*

<Stmt>
  ::= <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
   | "if" <Expr> "then" (<Stmt>)+ "else" (<Stmt>)+ "fi" <Expr>
   | "from" <Expr> "do"   (<Stmt>)+ "loop" (<Stmt>)+ "until" <Expr>
   | "local" <LocalDeclList> (<Stmt>)+ "delocal" <LocalDeclList>
   | "call" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | "uncall" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | <Lval> "<=>" <Lval>
   | "skip"

<ModOp> ::= "+=" | "-=" | "^=" | "*=" | "/="

<ArgList> ::= <Expr> ("," <Expr>)*

<Lval> ::= <Ident> [ "[" <Expr> "]" ]

<Expr> ::= <Expr> <BinOp> <Expr> | ("!" | "~") <Expr> | <Term>
<Term> ::= "(" <Expr> ")" | <Integer> | <Lval>
<BinOp> ::= "*" | "/" | "%" | "+" | "-" | "<=" | "<" | ">=" | ">" | "=" | "!=" | "^" | "&&" | "||"

<Ident> ::= <letter> (<alphaNum> | "_" | "'")*
<IdentNonMain> ::= <Ident>
<Integer> ::= <decimal-integer>
```

Static Reversibility Constraints (well-formedness rules)

```
C1. Reversible assignment (non-self-referential update)
    For any statement:
        <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
    let Base(<Lval>) be the identifier at the root of the lval
    (i.e., Base(x)=x, Base(a[i])=a).
    Then Base(<Lval>) MUST NOT occur in <Expr> as an identifier
    (neither as a variable nor as an array base).

          Examples:
            OK:   i += 1
            OK:   a[i] += 1
            NG:   i += i
            NG:   a[i] += a[j]

C2. If/fi consistency (entry/exit condition discipline)
    For:
        if E_in then S1 else S2 fi E_out
    forward execution must satisfy:
      - If E_in is true: execute S1 and after S1, E_out must be true.
      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.
    (In particular, E_in and E_out must be chosen so that the branch taken
     forward can be reconstructed backward.)

C3. From/until loop consistency + execution order (strengthened)
    For:
        from E_in do S1 loop S2 until E_out

    Execution order (forward):
      1) Evaluate E_in. It MUST hold at loop entry.
      2) Execute S1 once (if absent, treat as skip).
      3) Evaluate E_out.
         - If E_out is true, terminate the loop immediately.
         - If E_out is false, execute S2 once (if absent, treat as skip),
           then evaluate E_in and it MUST be false, and go back to step (2).

C4. local/delocal discipline (static well-formedness)

    For any block:
        local D1, ..., Dk   S   delocal D1', ..., Dk'

    the following must hold:

      (a) Arity agreement:
          The number of declarations must be the same:
              k = k'.

      (b) Identifier agreement:
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

      (c) Scope discipline:
          The identifiers introduced by the local declarations
          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

    Intuition:
      The local/delocal construct introduces temporary variables
      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
      while the delocal declarations specify the values
      required to re-enter the block during backward execution.

C5. Scope / declared-identifier discipline
    A program may use only identifiers that are in scope.
      - In a procedure body: identifiers must be procedure parameters or locals
        introduced by an enclosing local/delocal block.
      - In main: identifiers must be declared by earlier <MainVdecl> or locals.
      - No implicit globals.
      - No shadowing: a local declaration MUST NOT reuse an identifier already in scope.

C6. delocal expected-value check (runtime)
    For:
        local int x = E_in   S   delocal int x = E_out
    runtime check at delocal:
      - Immediately before delocal, the implementation checks:
            x == Eval(E_out)
        If it fails, abort.
    Note: E_out need not equal E_in.

      Examples:
            NG:   E_in is x
            NG:   E_out is x

C7. Semantics of the reversible update operators:
    - `x *= e` multiplies x by e and is undone by `x /= e`; it requires e != 0.
    - `x /= e` requires e != 0 and that x is exactly divisible by e (no remainder).
```

Single-line comments start with //; multi-line comments start with /* and end with */.

- A comment is written in any form other than // or /* ... */.
- A non-existent keyword such as 'end' is used.
- The keyword 'end' appears at the end of a procedure.

```
<Program> ::= (<ProcedureDef>)* <MainProcedureDef>
<ProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" <IdentNonMain> "(" [ <Param> ("," <Param>)* ] ")" (<Stmt>)+
<MainProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" "main" "(" ")" (<MainVdecl>)* (<Stmt>)+

<Param> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "[" "]" ]

<MainVdecl> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "=" <Expr> ]  | "int" <Ident> "[" "]" [ "=" <ArrayLit> ]
<ArrayLit> ::= "{" <Expr> ("," <Expr>)* "}"

<LocalDecl> ::= "int" <Ident> "=" <Expr>
<LocalDeclList> ::= <LocalDecl> ("," <LocalDecl>)*

<Stmt>
  ::= <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
   | "if" <Expr> "then" (<Stmt>)+ "else" (<Stmt>)+ "fi" <Expr>
   | "from" <Expr> "do"   (<Stmt>)+ "loop" (<Stmt>)+ "until" <Expr>
   | "local" <LocalDeclList> (<Stmt>)+ "delocal" <LocalDeclList>
   | "call" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | "uncall" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | <Lval> "<=>" <Lval>
   | "skip"

<ModOp> ::= "+=" | "-=" | "^=" | "*=" | "/="

<ArgList> ::= <Expr> ("," <Expr>)*

<Lval> ::= <Ident> [ "[" <Expr> "]" ]

<Expr> ::= <Expr> <BinOp> <Expr> | ("!" | "~") <Expr> | <Term>
<Term> ::= "(" <Expr> ")" | <Integer> | <Lval>
<BinOp> ::= "*" | "/" | "%" | "+" | "-" | "<=" | "<" | ">=" | ">" | "=" | "!=" | "^" | "&&" | "||"

<Ident> ::= <letter> (<alphaNum> | "_" | "'")*
<IdentNonMain> ::= <Ident>
<Integer> ::= <decimal-integer>
```

Static Reversibility Constraints (well-formedness rules)

```
C1. Reversible assignment (non-self-referential update)
    For any statement:
        <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
    let Base(<Lval>) be the identifier at the root of the lval
    (i.e., Base(x)=x, Base(a[i])=a).
    Then Base(<Lval>) MUST NOT occur in <Expr> as an identifier
    (neither as a variable nor as an array base).

          Examples:
            OK:   i += 1
            OK:   a[i] += 1
            NG:   i += i
            NG:   a[i] += a[j]

C2. If/fi consistency (entry/exit condition discipline)
    For:
        if E_in then S1 else S2 fi E_out
    forward execution must satisfy:
      - If E_in is true: execute S1 and after S1, E_out must be true.
      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.
    (In particular, E_in and E_out must be chosen so that the branch taken
     forward can be reconstructed backward.)

C3. From/until loop consistency + execution order (strengthened)
    For:
        from E_in do S1 loop S2 until E_out

    Execution order (forward):
      1) Evaluate E_in. It MUST hold at loop entry.
      2) Execute S1 once (if absent, treat as skip).
      3) Evaluate E_out.
         - If E_out is true, terminate the loop immediately.
         - If E_out is false, execute S2 once (if absent, treat as skip),
           then evaluate E_in and it MUST be false, and go back to step (2).

C4. local/delocal discipline (static well-formedness)

    For any block:
        local D1, ..., Dk   S   delocal D1', ..., Dk'

    the following must hold:

      (a) Arity agreement:
          The number of declarations must be the same:
              k = k'.

      (b) Identifier agreement:
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

      (c) Scope discipline:
          The identifiers introduced by the local declarations
          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

    Intuition:
      The local/delocal construct introduces temporary variables
      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
      while the delocal declarations specify the values
      required to re-enter the block during backward execution.

C5. Scope / declared-identifier discipline
    A program may use only identifiers that are in scope.
      - In a procedure body: identifiers must be procedure parameters or locals
        introduced by an enclosing local/delocal block.
      - In main: identifiers must be declared by earlier <MainVdecl> or locals.
      - No implicit globals.
      - No shadowing: a local declaration MUST NOT reuse an identifier already in scope.

C6. delocal expected-value check (runtime)
    For:
        local int x = E_in   S   delocal int x = E_out
    runtime check at delocal:
      - Immediately before delocal, the implementation checks:
            x == Eval(E_out)
        If it fails, abort.
    Note: E_out need not equal E_in.

      Examples:
            NG:   E_in is x
            NG:   E_out is x

C7. Semantics of the reversible update operators:
    - `x *= e` multiplies x by e and is undone by `x /= e`; it requires e != 0.
    - `x /= e` requires e != 0 and that x is exactly divisible by e (no remainder).
```

The procedure should: Encode the pair (x, y) into a single number with the Cantor pairing function: z must become (x+y)*(x+y+1)/2 + y. z starts at 0. After execution x and y must both be 0 (the pair is consumed into z; the pairing is a bijection, so no information is lost). Assume x >= 0 and y >= 0.

Example: when called with x=2, y=3, z=0, the expected result is x=0, y=0, z=18.

Output rules:
- Output ONLY the Janus procedure definition(s), starting with 'procedure'.
- Do NOT include main() or any global variable declarations.
- Do NOT include any explanations outside the code.
- Do NOT add any comments.
- Single-line comments start with //; multi-line comments start with /* and end with */.

```
<Program> ::= (<ProcedureDef>)* <MainProcedureDef>
<ProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" <IdentNonMain> "(" [ <Param> ("," <Param>)* ] ")" (<Stmt>)+
<MainProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" "main" "(" ")" (<MainVdecl>)* (<Stmt>)+

<Param> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "[" "]" ]

<MainVdecl> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "=" <Expr> ]  | "int" <Ident> "[" "]" [ "=" <ArrayLit> ]
<ArrayLit> ::= "{" <Expr> ("," <Expr>)* "}"

<LocalDecl> ::= "int" <Ident> "=" <Expr>
<LocalDeclList> ::= <LocalDecl> ("," <LocalDecl>)*

<Stmt>
  ::= <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
   | "if" <Expr> "then" (<Stmt>)+ "else" (<Stmt>)+ "fi" <Expr>
   | "from" <Expr> "do"   (<Stmt>)+ "loop" (<Stmt>)+ "until" <Expr>
   | "local" <LocalDeclList> (<Stmt>)+ "delocal" <LocalDeclList>
   | "call" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | "uncall" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | <Lval> "<=>" <Lval>
   | "skip"

<ModOp> ::= "+=" | "-=" | "^=" | "*=" | "/="

<ArgList> ::= <Expr> ("," <Expr>)*

<Lval> ::= <Ident> [ "[" <Expr> "]" ]

<Expr> ::= <Expr> <BinOp> <Expr> | ("!" | "~") <Expr> | <Term>
<Term> ::= "(" <Expr> ")" | <Integer> | <Lval>
<BinOp> ::= "*" | "/" | "%" | "+" | "-" | "<=" | "<" | ">=" | ">" | "=" | "!=" | "^" | "&&" | "||"

<Ident> ::= <letter> (<alphaNum> | "_" | "'")*
<IdentNonMain> ::= <Ident>
<Integer> ::= <decimal-integer>
```

Static Reversibility Constraints (well-formedness rules)

```
C1. Reversible assignment (non-self-referential update)
    For any statement:
        <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
    let Base(<Lval>) be the identifier at the root of the lval
    (i.e., Base(x)=x, Base(a[i])=a).
    Then Base(<Lval>) MUST NOT occur in <Expr> as an identifier
    (neither as a variable nor as an array base).

          Examples:
            OK:   i += 1
            OK:   a[i] += 1
            NG:   i += i
            NG:   a[i] += a[j]

C2. If/fi consistency (entry/exit condition discipline)
    For:
        if E_in then S1 else S2 fi E_out
    forward execution must satisfy:
      - If E_in is true: execute S1 and after S1, E_out must be true.
      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.
    (In particular, E_in and E_out must be chosen so that the branch taken
     forward can be reconstructed backward.)

C3. From/until loop consistency + execution order (strengthened)
    For:
        from E_in do S1 loop S2 until E_out

    Execution order (forward):
      1) Evaluate E_in. It MUST hold at loop entry.
      2) Execute S1 once (if absent, treat as skip).
      3) Evaluate E_out.
         - If E_out is true, terminate the loop immediately.
         - If E_out is false, execute S2 once (if absent, treat as skip),
           then evaluate E_in and it MUST be false, and go back to step (2).

C4. local/delocal discipline (static well-formedness)

    For any block:
        local D1, ..., Dk   S   delocal D1', ..., Dk'

    the following must hold:

      (a) Arity agreement:
          The number of declarations must be the same:
              k = k'.

      (b) Identifier agreement:
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

      (c) Scope discipline:
          The identifiers introduced by the local declarations
          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

    Intuition:
      The local/delocal construct introduces temporary variables
      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
      while the delocal declarations specify the values
      required to re-enter the block during backward execution.

C5. Scope / declared-identifier discipline
    A program may use only identifiers that are in scope.
      - In a procedure body: identifiers must be procedure parameters or locals
        introduced by an enclosing local/delocal block.
      - In main: identifiers must be declared by earlier <MainVdecl> or locals.
      - No implicit globals.
      - No shadowing: a local declaration MUST NOT reuse an identifier already in scope.

C6. delocal expected-value check (runtime)
    For:

    runtime check at delocal:
      - Immediately before delocal, the implementation checks:
            x == Eval(E_out)
        If it fails, abort.
    Note: E_out need not equal E_in.

      Examples:
            NG:   E_in is x
            NG:   E_out is x

C7. Semantics of the reversible update operators:
    - `x *= e` multiplies x by e and is undone by `x /= e`; it requires e != 0.
    - `x /= e` requires e != 0 and that x is exactly divisible by e (no remainder).
```

```
C2. If/fi consistency (entry/exit condition discipline)
    For:
        if E_in then S1 else S2 fi E_out
    forward execution must satisfy:
      - If E_in is true: execute S1 and after S1, E_out must be true.
      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.
    (In particular, E_in and E_out must be chosen so that the branch taken
     forward can be reconstructed backward.)

    For:
        from E_in do S1 loop S2 until E_out

    Execution order (forward):
      1) Evaluate E_in. It MUST hold at loop entry.
      2) Execute S1 once (if absent, treat as skip).
      3) Evaluate E_out.
         - If E_out is true, terminate the loop immediately.
         - If E_out is false, execute S2 once (if absent, treat as skip),

        local D1, ..., Dk   S   delocal D1', ..., Dk'

    the following must hold:

      (b) Identifier agreement:
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

      (c) Scope discipline:
          The identifiers introduced by the local declarations
          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

    Intuition:
      The local/delocal construct introduces temporary variables
      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
      while the delocal declarations specify the values
      required to re-enter the block during backward execution.
```

```
<MainProcedureDef> ::= "procedure" "main" "(" ")" (<MainVdecl>)* (<Stmt>)+

<Param> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "[" "]" ]

<MainVdecl> ::= "int" <Ident> [ "=" <Expr> ]  | "int" <Ident> "[" "]" [ "=" <ArrayLit> ]
<ArrayLit> ::= "{" <Expr> ("," <Expr>)* "}"

<LocalDecl> ::= "int" <Ident> "=" <Expr>
<LocalDeclList> ::= <LocalDecl> ("," <LocalDecl>)*

<Stmt>
  ::= <Lval> <ModOp> <Expr>
   | "if" <Expr> "then" (<Stmt>)+ "else" (<Stmt>)+ "fi" <Expr>
   | "from" <Expr> "do"   (<Stmt>)+ "loop" (<Stmt>)+ "until" <Expr>
   | "local" <LocalDeclList> (<Stmt>)+ "delocal" <LocalDeclList>
   | "call" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | "uncall" <Ident> "(" [ <ArgList> ] ")"
   | <Lval> "<=>" <Lval>
   | "skip"

<ModOp> ::= "+=" | "-=" | "^=" | "*=" | "/="

<ArgList> ::= <Expr> ("," <Expr>)*

<Lval> ::= <Ident> [ "[" <Expr> "]" ]

<Expr> ::= <Expr> <BinOp> <Expr> | ("!" | "~") <Expr> | <Term>
<Term> ::= "(" <Expr> ")" | <Integer> | <Lval>
<BinOp> ::= "*" | "/" | "%" | "+" | "-" | "<=" | "<" | ">=" | ">" | "=" | "!=" | "^" | "&&" | "||"

```
```
C1. Reversible assignment (non-self-referential update)

    Then Base(<Lval>) MUST NOT occur in <Expr> as an identifier
    (neither as a variable nor as an array base).

          Examples:
            OK:   i += 1
            OK:   a[i] += 1
            NG:   i += i
            NG:   a[i] += a[j]

C2. If/fi consistency (entry/exit condition discipline)
    For:
        if E_in then S1 else S2 fi E_out
    forward execution must satisfy:
      - If E_in is true: execute S1 and after S1, E_out must be true.
      - If E_in is false: execute S2 and after S2, E_out must be false.
    (In particular, E_in and E_out must be chosen so that the branch taken
     forward can be reconstructed backward.)

    For:
        from E_in do S1 loop S2 until E_out

    Execution order (forward):
      1) Evaluate E_in. It MUST hold at loop entry.
      2) Execute S1 once (if absent, treat as skip).
      3) Evaluate E_out.

         - If E_out is false, execute S2 once (if absent, treat as skip),

    For any block:
        local D1, ..., Dk   S   delocal D1', ..., Dk'

    the following must hold:

      (a) Arity agreement:

              k = k'.

      (b) Identifier agreement:
          The declared identifiers must match exactly,
          in the same order and with the same names.
          (Only the initializer expressions may differ.)

      (c) Scope discipline:
          The identifiers introduced by the local declarations
          are in scope only within S.
          They must not be accessed outside the block.

    Intuition:
      The local/delocal construct introduces temporary variables
      whose lifetimes are statically delimited,
      while the delocal declarations specify the values
      required to re-enter the block during backward execution.

C5. Scope / declared-identifier discipline
    A program may use only identifiers that are in scope.
      - In a procedure body: identifiers must be procedure parameters or locals

      - In main: identifiers must be declared by earlier <MainVdecl> or locals.
      - No implicit globals.
      - No shadowing: a local declaration MUST NOT reuse an identifier already in scope.

      - Immediately before delocal, the implementation checks:
            x == Eval(E_out)
        If it fails, abort.
    Note: E_out need not equal E_in.

      Examples:
            NG:   E_in is x
            NG:   E_out is x

C7. Semantics of the reversible update operators:
    - `x *= e` multiplies x by e and is undone by `x /= e`; it requires e != 0.
```

```
   | <Lval> "<=>" <Lval>
   | "skip"

<Lval> ::= <Ident> [ "[" <Expr> "]" ]

<Expr> ::= <Expr> <BinOp> <Expr> | ("!" | "~") <Expr> | <Term>
<Term> ::= "(" <Expr> ")" | <Integer> | <Lval>
<BinOp> ::= "*" | "/" | "%" | "+" | "-" | "<=" | "<" | ">=" | ">" | "=" | "!=" | "^" | "&&" | "||"

<Ident> ::= <letter> (<alphaNum> | "_" | "'")*

<Integer> ::= <decimal-integer>
```
